FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, longitudinal, baseline ages 9-10, passive sensing via EARS app, cross-sectional data reported from 2019-2021

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis validating passively-sensed smartphone measures (keyboard use, app use); reports reliability, correlations, temporal patterns

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published paper in Scientific Reports (2024)

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper is a measurement/validation study of smartphone sensing methods. Describes passive measures and documents trends in youth smartphone use, but does NOT pair smartphone use with any individual-trait-level variable (neural, behavioral, mental health, cognitive, well-being, sleep, etc.) as IV or DV. Smartphone is described as a construct to be measured, not analyzed in relation to outcomes.

notes: Excluded at FT-IC2. Although paper reports original passive sensing validation of smartphone use in ABCD, it lacks analysis pairing smartphone use with individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, described as groundbreaking study of 11,876 youth ages 9-10, 21 U.S. sites, longitudinal design

FT_IC5.evidence: Paper is a resource/tool description and documentation article describing NowIKnowMyABCD (GitHub discussion board and website for ABCD community). No original empirical analysis of ABCD data; describes methods and infrastructure for code-sharing, tutorials, and community support.

notes: Excluded at FT-IC5. Paper describes a community resource platform and documentation hub for ABCD researchers but does not report original empirical analysis of ABCD data.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=11,868 baseline (ages ~9/10), followed to ages ~13/14, 21 U.S. sites, longitudinal design across 5 waves

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using descriptive statistics and multilevel logistic regression to examine longitudinal trajectories and patterns of technology use subtypes

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published paper in Journal of Adolescent Health (2024)

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper examines 6 technology subtypes (social media, texting, video chat, TV/movies, video games, YouTube) but does NOT pair any with individual-trait-level variables. All analyses report associations between technology use and DEMOGRAPHIC/CONTEXTUAL covariates only: biological sex, parental education, parental income, parental marital status. Other-side variables are not within-person traits (neural, behavioral, mental health, cognitive, well-being, sleep, academic, etc.) but purely demographic and family structure. Paper lacks any analysis linking technology use to individual-level health or developmental outcomes.

notes: Excluded at FT-IC2. Although paper reports original longitudinal analysis of technology use in ABCD with quantitative results and is published peer-reviewed, the outcomes are exclusively demographic/sociodemographic covariates, not individual-trait variables. No pairing of smartphone/social media with neural, behavioral, mental health, or similar individual-trait outcomes.
